sub: Where your collision-avoidance code gets to fail safely

title: Why Simulate?
desc: Virtual test environments for drone flights and real-time simulation

items: You cannot flight-test a deconfliction algorithm against 200 real aircraft — simulation is the only safe laboratory
Virtual test environments reproduce airspace structure, traffic and aircraft performance models
Real-time simulation lets humans and systems interact with the scenario as it unfolds; fast-time simulation runs thousands of scenarios overnight
Remember Module 4: verification evidence under normal and fault conditions — simulation is how you produce it cheaply
Scenario coverage: rare events (link loss, sensor failure, rogue drones) can be triggered on demand

title: Two Simulators, Two Questions
desc: This course uses the word "simulator" for two very different things — know which one you need

rows: The actual flight controller, SDK and telemetry path — only physics and GPS are synthetic
Nothing — a pure Python traffic model
Required — the drone sits on your desk, props off
Runaway virtual-stick loops, bad state handling, wrong units
Other traffic — there is only ever one aircraft
Whether your app works on real hardware at all

items: Neither replaces the other: the DJI simulator validates the client, BlueSky validates the traffic logic — Module 4's evidence under normal and fault conditions applied at two different levels
Project idea #14 is exactly this bridge — inject the real drone into BlueSky as an aircraft, or run a sim scenario alongside a real flight (Module 2)
Rule of thumb: anything that moves a real motor gets the DJI simulator first; anything that decides where aircraft go gets BlueSky first

title: Test Environment vs. Reality
desc: What simulation can and cannot promise you

body.alt: MonkeyUser comic: a smooth flat test-environment track ends in a cliff into a production canyon full of spikes, lag hills and performance drops
body.caption: monkeyuser.com — Testing vs. Production Environment. Simulation is your smooth track; the real airspace is the canyon. Validate in BlueSky first.

title: BlueSky: The Open ATM Simulator
desc: TU Delft's open-source air traffic simulator — and our competition arena

items: Open source (GPL), written in Python — easy to read, easy to extend; used in actual ATM research at TU Delft and beyond
Scenario scripting, aircraft performance models, radar-style visualization
A plugin architecture — including the asas plugins for conflict detection & resolution

title: BlueSky Radar View
desc: Dense traffic over the Balkans — every green tag is an aircraft your plugin must keep apart

body.alt: BlueSky simulator radar view with dense air traffic over the Balkans
body.caption: Traffic simulation using the BlueSky simulator.

title: Competition Setup: Your Turn
desc: Build a collision-avoidance system, survive longer than everyone else

body.code: # get the simulator pip install bluesky-simulator[full] # or from source: git clone https://github.com/TUDelft-CNS-ATM/bluesky.git # start the GUI bluesky # where your work goes bluesky/plugins/ <-- your plugin lives here bluesky/plugins/asas/ <-- study these first

items: Work in your groups from Module 2 — one plugin per group
Same traffic scenario for everyone; your system steers the drones
Scoring: time until first collision — the longest-surviving airspace wins
Honest engineering beats parameter tuning: be ready to explain your approach (detection horizon, resolution maneuver, priorities)
